whatTheyDo: Makes optical transceivers that convert electrical signals into light pulses for high-speed data center connections.
whyNVDA: GPU racks at scale require optical links to move data fast enough between chips — copper cables physically cannot keep up at this bandwidth.

whatTheyDo: Produces industrial lasers and optical components used inside data center transceivers and telecom equipment.
whyNVDA: Their laser components are inside the optical modules that connect NVDA GPU clusters across hyperscaler data centers.

whatTheyDo: Builds high-speed optical transceivers and laser components for AI data centers and telecommunications infrastructure.
whyNVDA: One of very few companies that can supply 400G/800G/1.6T optical modules at the volume AI infrastructure buildout demands.

whatTheyDo: Swedish pure-play in silicon photonics, mmWave chips, and next-generation optical interconnects designed for AI data centers.
whyNVDA: Next-gen Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) — integrating optical directly onto chip packages — depends on silicon photonics specialists like Sivers.

whatTheyDo: US-based manufacturer of DRAM, NAND flash, and High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) for AI accelerator chips.
whyNVDA: Every NVDA H100/H200/B200 GPU has HBM physically stacked on it — Micron is one of only 3 companies worldwide that can make it.

whatTheyDo: South Korean memory giant and currently the world's leading supplier of HBM3 chips used in NVDA flagship AI GPUs.
whyNVDA: SK Hynix supplies the majority of HBM inside NVDA H100/H200 chips — arguably NVDA's single most critical supply chain partner right now.

whatTheyDo: Makes compound semiconductor substrates — indium phosphide and gallium arsenide wafers used as the base for optical and RF chips.
whyNVDA: Their specialty wafer substrates are the foundation material for many photonic and high-frequency chips throughout the AI supply chain.

whatTheyDo: Designs the world's most powerful AI GPUs (H100, H200, B200) and the CUDA software platform that runs all major AI models.
whyNVDA: NVDA IS the center of this entire map — every company here either supplies into NVDA chips, physically makes them, connects them, or builds on top of them.

whatTheyDo: Israeli specialty analog and mixed-signal foundry that manufactures niche chips that large-scale foundries like TSMC will not produce.
whyNVDA: Produces analog, RF, and sensing chips — critical supporting components for photonics, power management, and AI infrastructure hardware.

whatTheyDo: Makes x86 CPUs and is building Intel Foundry Services (IFS) as a US-based alternative contract chip manufacturer to TSMC.
whyNVDA: Potential US-based alternative foundry for advanced chip packaging; also competes in AI accelerators with its Gaudi product line.

whatTheyDo: The world's dominant contract chip foundry, manufacturing virtually every leading-edge chip including all NVIDIA GPU designs.
whyNVDA: NVDA is a fabless company — TSMC physically makes 100% of NVDA GPUs. No TSMC capacity = no NVIDIA chips, period.

whatTheyDo: European-origin GPU cloud provider building large-scale NVDA GPU clusters and renting AI compute capacity to developers and enterprises.
whyNVDA: Neoclouds are among NVDA's fastest-growing direct customer segment — they commit to large GPU orders and deploy them immediately.

whatTheyDo: US-based neocloud operating massive NVDA GPU clusters, recently IPO'd, and one of NVDA's biggest revenue customers.
whyNVDA: CoreWeave has committed billions in NVDA GPU purchases — a direct, concentrated demand driver for NVDA data center revenue.

whatTheyDo: Makes semiconductor wafer-level burn-in and test equipment used to stress-test and qualify chips before they ship to customers.
whyNVDA: As AI chips become more complex and expensive (H100 costs over $30K each), catching defects before deployment is critical — Aehr tools are used on advanced logic and silicon carbide chips.

whatTheyDo: French company that produces Silicon-on-Insulator (SOI) and specialty semiconductor wafers used in RF chips, photonics, and power devices.
whyNVDA: SOI wafers are used in RF and photonics chips throughout the AI supply chain — a materials-layer play on the photonics and advanced packaging buildout.

whatTheyDo: German company producing precision laser systems used to structure printed circuit boards and create the interconnect layers in advanced chip packaging substrates.
whyNVDA: NVDA chips use advanced CoWoS packaging from TSMC — the substrate layers in these packages require precision laser structuring tools like LPKF's systems.
